statement: The Sun's absolute bolometric magnitude is conventionally centered at about 4.74 mag. As a tight calibration prior for stellar-luminosity work, model M_bol_sun with a Normal distribution whose small width represents practical calibration uncertainty around the IAU bolometric zero point.
source: IAU 2015 Resolution B2/B3 on bolometric magnitude zero points and nominal solar luminosity

statement: For old, metal-poor RR Lyrae stars near [Fe/H] = -1.5, a common visual absolute-magnitude calibration is approximately 0.60 mag. A Normal prior with 0.10 mag width captures metallicity, evolutionary, and calibration scatter for distance-scale applications.
source: Catelan & Smith, Pulsating Stars, standard RR Lyrae calibration review

statement: For a fundamental-mode classical Cepheid with orbital pulsation period P = 10 days, equivalently log10 P = 1, the visual absolute magnitude is about -4.05 mag. The stated Normal width covers intrinsic instability-strip scatter, metallicity effects, and calibration uncertainty.
source: Freedman & Madore review of Cepheid period-luminosity calibration and the HST Key Project distance scale

statement: For roughly solar-type main-sequence stars, about 0.5 to 2 solar masses, the textbook scaling L/L_sun approximately equals (M/M_sun)^alpha. A Normal prior centered on alpha = 3.5 with width 0.5 is appropriate for approximate stellar-population modeling.
source: Carroll & Ostlie, An Introduction to Modern Astrophysics

statement: For field solar-type binary systems, the empirical orbital-period distribution is well approximated by log10(P/day) following Normal(5.03, 2.28). In PyMC's natural-log LogNormal parameterization, this corresponds to P in days following LogNormal(mu = 11.58, sigma = 5.25).
source: Raghavan et al. nearby solar-type stellar multiplicity survey

statement: For close-in giant exoplanets classified as hot Jupiters, the observed period pile-up is near 3 days. A LogNormal prior on P in days with natural-log parameters mu = 1.10 and sigma = 0.45 concentrates most probability between roughly 2 and 5 days while allowing a broader hot-Jupiter tail.
source: Winn & Fabrycky review of exoplanet architectures and hot-Jupiter period distributions

statement: The Earth's sidereal orbital period around the Sun is tightly known at approximately 365.25636 days. For ephemeris-level probabilistic modeling where the period is represented as a stochastic parameter, a very narrow Normal prior is appropriate.
source: The Astronomical Almanac, standard solar-system ephemeris reference

statement: The extinction-corrected true distance modulus of the Large Magellanic Cloud is tightly anchored by detached eclipsing binaries. A practical prior for distance-ladder work is Normal with mean 18.477 mag and standard deviation 0.026 mag.
source: Pietrzynski et al. detached-eclipsing-binary distance to the Large Magellanic Cloud

statement: The true distance modulus to M31, the Andromeda Galaxy, is commonly taken near 24.47 mag, corresponding to a distance of about 785 kpc. A Normal prior with 0.07 mag width captures the standard Local Group distance-scale uncertainty.
source: McConnachie 2012 Local Group galaxy properties review

statement: The distance modulus corresponding to the modern Galactic-center distance R0 near 8.18 kpc is about 14.56 mag. A Normal prior with 0.03 mag width captures contemporary S-star orbital constraints and residual systematic uncertainty.
source: GRAVITY Collaboration Galactic-center distance measurements from S2 and other S-star orbits

statement: The Hyades open cluster is a nearby distance-ladder calibrator with true distance modulus close to 3.33 mag. Hipparcos and Gaia parallax work support a tight Normal prior with 0.02 mag standard deviation.
source: Perryman et al. Hipparcos Hyades distance calibration, standard open-cluster parallax reference

statement: Under the flat Lambda-CDM model fitted to cosmic microwave background anisotropy data, the Hubble constant is about 67.4 km s^-1 Mpc^-1. This is a Planck-like cosmology prior rather than a method-agnostic consensus prior, because of the well-known Hubble-tension literature.
